paragraphs.text: Means ‘I understand.’ You can say this to show understanding or empathy.
Pailin: I left that job even though the pay was great because I was miserable. [audio:phrases_verbs_i_get_it_2]
Luke: I get it, money isn't everything if you're unhappy every single day.
Pailin: I still think about him even though I know it’s over. [audio:phrases_verbs_i_get_it_3]
Luke: I get it. The feelings don’t just disappear just because the relationship is over.
A phrase used to smoothly transition into a related topic that was just mentioned or implied in the conversation. It connects what was just said to something new you want to bring up.
I really need to start working out more consistently. Speaking of which, did you see they just opened up a new gym down the street? [audio:phrases_verbs_speaking_of_which_2]

You really should consider moving into my neighborhood, I think you’d like it. Oh, speaking of which, Britney’s actually moving into my apartment building next month! [audio:phrases_verbs_speaking_of_which_3]
A way to agree to a proposal or compromise. It’s like saying ‘We have an agreement.’
Pailin: I’ll cook if you do the dishes. [audio:phrases_verbs_deal_2]
Luke: How about I drive us to the beach, and you pay for parking. [audio:phrases_verbs_deal_3]
An informal adjective to describe someone or something that’s fancy, expensive, upscale, or pretentious. Short for the French word ‘bourgeois.’
Let’s go to a different restaurant. This one looks too bougie. [audio:phrases_verbs_bougie_2]
Hotels in Chiang Mai are so cheap! We should take this opportunity to stay at a super bougie hotel. [audio:phrases_verbs_bougie_3]
A phrase used to show that there’s a situation or even happening, usually one that’s unclear or noteworthy.
Was there something going on at the mall? It was packed today! [audio:phrases_verbs_going_on_2]
There’s a festival going on downtown this weekend if you want to check it out. [audio:phrases_verbs_going_on_3]
Judy’s in Mongolia right now? Dang, it seems like she’s always traveling! I’m so jealous. [audio:phrases_verbs_dang1_3]
To stay private, avoid socializing, or not share personal thoughts with others. It describes someone who’s private, shy, or introverted.
She’s not unfriendly, she just keeps to herself and people sometimes mistake that for being a cold person. [audio:phrases_verbs_keep_to_myself_2]
I’m normally really loud and outgoing, but I keep to myself at work because I don’t need my coworkers prying into my private life. [audio:phrases_verbs_keep_to_myself_3]
A casual way of saying ‘this is normal.’ It’s used to reassure someone that a mistake or mishap isn’t worth stressing over.
Pailin: I’m so sorry, I know we’ve met several times but I can’t remember your name! [audio:phrases_verbs_it_happens_2]
Man: Don’t worry, it happens. I’m Jacob.
Pailin: Oh no, I sent that email to the wrong person. [audio:phrases_verbs_it_happens_3]
Boss: It happens, just follow up and explain to them you sent it by mistake.
An exclamation to show mild frustration or disappointment.
Oh, shoot, I forgot my wallet at home. [audio:phrases_verbs_shoot_2]
Shoot, I have a doctor’s appointment in 30 minutes! I totally forgot about it. [audio:phrases_verbs_shoot_3]
Said when something is so surprising, annoying, or unbelievable that you can hardly accept that it’s real.
Luke: You’re kidding me. What are we supposed to do?
Luke: Heather got the job at Apple. [audio:phrases_verbs_youre_kidding_me_3]
Pailin: You’re kidding me! She has barely any work experience!
A casual way of saying that you’re enthusiastic to do something that someone has suggested.
Pailin: Want to grab tacos after work? [audio:phrases_verbs_im_down_2]
Luke: Do you want to come with me to a costume party this Saturday? [audio:phrases_verbs_im_down_3]
Pailin: I’m so down, I love costume parties.
A WEIGHT HAS BEEN LIFTED OFF MY SHOULDERS
A weight has been lifted off my shoulders [audio:phrases_verbs_a_weight_has_been_lifted_1]
An expression to mean the feeling of relief when something you've been stressed or worried about is finally over or resolved.
I finally paid off my student loans after 20 years! It feels like a huge weight’s been lifted off my shoulders. [audio:phrases_verbs_a_weight_has_been_lifted_2]
I quit that toxic job today. I’m so glad I’ll never have to step foot in that office again. It’s like a weight has been lifted off my shoulders. [audio:phrases_verbs_a_weight_has_been_lifted_3]
To be noticeably different or better than others. To catch people's attention in a positive way.
He always stands out in a crowd because he always wears funky outfits. [audio:phrases_verbs_stand_out_2]
I looked through all the resumes, and there’s one that really stood out to me. I think we should call her in for an interview. [audio:phrases_verbs_stand_out_3]
An expression meaning to take on different roles or responsibilities in a job.
We want to hire an assistant for our startup. They’ve got to be willing to wear different hats. [audio:phrases_verbs_wear_different_hats_2]
As a small business owner, I’m used to wearing different hats. [audio:phrases_verbs_wear_different_hats_3]
When an alarm, bell, or notification suddenly makes a sound.
My alarm went off at 6AM but I snoozed it three times. [audio:phrases_verbs_go_off_2]
I forgot to silence my phone and it went off during the meeting. [audio:phrases_verbs_go_off_3]
The exhaustion you feel after traveling across different time zones because your internal body clock hasn’t adjusted to the new time yet.
The jet lag has been hitting me hard. I was wide awake at 3AM. [audio:phrases_verbs_jet_lagged_2]
I just flew in from Tokyo this morning, so I’m completely jet-lagged. [audio:phrases_verbs_jet_lagged_3]
To wake up later than usual, either on purpose or by accident.
Tomorrow’s Saturday, which means no work! I’m planning on sleeping in until 11AM. [audio:phrases_verbs_sleep_in_2]
We don’t have to be anywhere until noon, so feel free to sleep in. [audio:phrases_verbs_sleep_in_3]
To review or check something, usually quickly or casually.
I looked over the contract but I think you should have a lawyer read it too. [audio:phrases_verbs_look_over_2]
I just need five minutes to look over the report before the meeting. [audio:phrases_verbs_look_over_3]
To register your arrival somewhere, like a hotel, airport, event, etc.
We can check in at the hotel after 3. [audio:phrases_verbs_check_in_2]
I checked in online so I don’t have to wait in line at the airport. [audio:phrases_verbs_check_in_3]
Driving dangerously close behind another car, usually because you’re impatient with the driver in front for driving too slowly, or you’re angry at them and trying to intimidate them into moving over.
You’re going 40 miles per hour in a 50 zone! No wonder the guy behind you is tailgating you. [audio:phrases_verbs_tailgating_2]
Can this guy stop tailgating me? I can’t go any faster, there’s traffic on the freeway. [audio:phrases_verbs_tailgating_3]
In a driving context, it’s when another driver suddenly moves into your lane right in front of you, forcing you to brake or swerve. It’s usually done without warning.
Did you see that?? That guy just cut me off! [audio:phrases_verbs_cut_off_2]
Be careful, you just cut that person off! Check your mirrors next time! [audio:phrases_verbs_cut_off_3]
A casual exclamation to express a strong reaction, usually annoyance or being overwhelmed.
Sheesh, that test was way harder than I expected. [audio:phrases_verbs_sheesh_2]
Luke: Pai, is dinner ready yet? I’m starving. [audio:phrases_verbs_sheesh_3]
Pailin: I’m cooking as fast as I can, sheesh. You know, you could help me out to speed things up.
To cancel plans at the last minute or back out of something you committed to, often leaving someone disappointed or in a difficult spot.
Roger bailed on me the day of the concert. And I already bought us tickets! [audio:phrases_verbs_bail_on_2]
Sorry guys, but I might have to bail on tonight. I’m so exhausted. I don’t have the energy to socialize. [audio:phrases_verbs_bail_on_3]

Used to signal that you expect the listener to understand what you're implying, without having to explain it completely. It can add a tone of ‘I shouldn't have to explain this’ or ‘you get what I mean, right?’
You could’ve just told me the truth, you know. I would’ve understood. [audio:phrases_verbs_you_know3_2]
I hope you appreciate all the help I’m giving you. I don’t have to be here, you know. I could be doing other things with my free time. [audio:phrases_verbs_you_know3_3]
When used at the start of a sentence, it signals that you’re about to say something direct, serious, or honest, usually to defend yourself.
Pailin: You promised you’d be there. [audio:phrases_verbs_look_2]
Luke: Look, something came up. I couldn’t help it.
Girlfriend: Why do you always say mean things like that? [audio:phrases_verbs_look_3]
Boyfriend: Look, can we not talk about this right now? We’re on vacation, can we just enjoy our time together?
An adjective to describe when something is done without any real effort or enthusiasm. Your heart isn’t really in it.
He gave a half-hearted apology and expected everything to go back to normal. [audio:phrases_verbs_half_hearted_2]
I made a half-hearted attempt to clean the apartment before giving up. [audio:phrases_verbs_half_hearted_3]
Someone who’s at the top of their field that’s widely recognized, especially in the entertainment industry.
The party is gonna have a ton of A-list celebrities! [audio:phrases_verbs_a_list_2]
Luke: I can’t believe we saw Margot Robbie at that coffee shop! [audio:phrases_verbs_a_list_3]
Pailin: I know! I wasn’t expecting to see any A-list celebrities there.
Used to describe a beginning that’s going well. It can also be used sarcastically when things go wrong early on.
The new hire solved a huge problem of ours on her first day! She’s off to a great start. [audio:phrases_verbs_off_to_a_great_start_2]
Oh no, I spilled coffee all over my shirt! Wow, this day is off to a great start. [audio:phrases_verbs_off_to_a_great_start_3]
Can be used as a transition word to shift from one topic to another. It’s a soft signal to show you’re moving on to the next topic.
We’ve gone over the contract in detail. Now, are you comfortable with moving forward with the deal? [audio:phrases_verbs_now_2]
You’ve had a few days to settle into the new job. Now, how are you finding everything? [audio:phrases_verbs_now_3]
To guide someone step by step through a process, explanation, or situation, making sure they understand each part along the way.
I’ll walk you through the steps first, and then you can try it on your own. [audio:phrases_verbs_walk_you_through_2]
You got in a car accident? Okay, walk me through what happened. [audio:phrases_verbs_walk_you_through_3]
Pailin: I’ve never filed taxes before. [audio:phrases_verbs_walk_you_through_4]
Luke: Don’t worry, I’ll walk you through it.
To think of or produce an idea, solution, excuse, or plan, usually through creativity or effort.
How did you guys come up with the name Pailin Abroad for your website? [audio:phrases_verbs_come_up_with_2]
I couldn’t come up with a single excuse, so I just told her the truth. [audio:phrases_verbs_come_up_with_3]
I came up with a few ideas for our Halloween costume. Do you wanna hear them? [audio:phrases_verbs_come_up_with_4]
A casual way of telling someone not to worry or stress about something.
Pailin: Sorry Luke, but I have to cancel our lunch plans today. I’ve got a ton of homework I need to finish. [audio:phrases_verbs_dont_sweat_it_2]
Luke: Don’t sweat it, we can reschedule for another day.
Luke: Oops, I forgot to bring your phone charger! [audio:phrases_verbs_dont_sweat_it_3]
Pailin: Don’t sweat it. I’m sure someone here’s got one.
What are some common problems you have to deal with at work or school?
job, career, part time job, shift, work, manager, boss, museum, art museum, first day, feedback, problem, new job
